EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds impacts for metro and columbia river gorge including power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations measured gusts of 45 to 50 mph. Multiple reports of fallen trees on power lines. Hundreds of PGE customers without power in Clackamas, Washington and Multnomah Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front produced a burst of high winds along the North Oregon Coast on December 4, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind measuring sites measured high winds along the Oregon coast December 4th through December 5th. The peak measured wind was 71 mph at Garibaldi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense Fog in the Central Willamette Valley contributed to a aircraft accident. A small plane struck power pole and power lines near Independence, Oregon and crashed just outside airport grounds, killing all three people onboard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense Fog in the Central Willamette Valley contributed to deadly aircraft accident.  A single engine airplane travelling from McMinnville to Independence crashed around 4:55 PM into a power pole just outside of the Independence Airport. The crash resulted in a local power outage, a small brushfire and the loss of all three people on board the airplane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwesterly swell of 20 to 25 ft with a period of 17 seconds was observed across the coastal waters, resulting in a high threat of sneaker waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A southwesterly swell of 20 to 25 ft with a period of 17 seconds was observed across the coastal waters. A 5 year old girl was hospitalized after she was hit by a sneaker wave near South Jetty Beach in Florence, OR.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and accumulating hail overwhelmed storm drains along Sunnyside Road near SE 117th Ave, leading to several inches of standing water on the road. Water was near the top of wheel wells of some vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of moderate atmospheric rivers brought heavy rain leading to minor river and urban flooding to portions of northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported a rockslide on Highway 36, with small trees down, but could move around them. Tripcheck showed rockslide between Rainrock and Swisshome, OR. Areas of running water over Highway 36 and the train tracks was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river brought heavy rains on December 5th which pushed many streams and rivers to flood stage along the coast, with minor flooding on many smaller streams and creeks across northwest Oregon. After moderate rainfall December 1st through the 4th, a very slow moving front dumped more heavy rain to the northern Oregon coast and coast range mountains. Area of the Coast Range received 12 to 16 inches of rain over the 5 day period. Much of Tillamook County saw flooding impacts, including the Wilson and Trask Rivers, and the Nehalem River near Foss. Other rivers that also reached minor flood stage included the Luckiamute near Suver and the Siletz River at Siletz.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Luckiamute River near Suver as an atmospheric river brought heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river brought heavy rains on December 5th which pushed many streams and rivers to flood stage along the coast, with minor flooding on many smaller streams and creeks across northwest Oregon. After moderate rainfall December 1st through the 4th, a very slow moving front dumped more heavy rain to the northern Oregon coast and coast range mountains. Area of the Coast Range received 12 to 16 inches of rain over the 5 day period. Much of Tillamook County saw flooding impacts, including the Wilson and Trask Rivers, and the Nehalem River near Foss. Other rivers that also reached minor flood stage included the Luckiamute near Suver and the Siletz River at Siletz.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river brought heavy rains to the north Oregon coast on Dec 5th. Tillamook County EM reported Kilchis River just north of Tillamook had minor flooding. The Wilson River at Tillamook also remained in minor flood stage during a 30-hour period, cresting at 16.07 feet at 330 pm on December 5th. Flood stage is 12.0 feet. The Trask River also rose above minor flood stage, cresting at 17.0 feet at 10 pm on December 5th. Flood stage is 16.5 feet. Rockaway Beach in southern Tillamook County had localized flooding due to heavy rain. Manzanita in northern Tillamook County had a power outage probably due to downed trees on power lines. Miami Foley Road was washed out which put Highway 101 as the only direct route between Garibaldi and Mohler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave trough and strong cold-frontal boundary pushed through the region the morning of Feb 13th followed by a period post-frontal showers the rest of the 13th driven by anomalous cold air aloft. A secondary wave feature dropping southward from BC the night of the 13th into the 14th provided a period of substantially increased snow showers over the mountains, particularly the Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site MLKO3(Milk Shakes) recorded 18 inches over a 17 hour period ending at 0300 on Feb 14th, and the SNOTEL site HIRO3(High Ridge) recorded 12 inches in a 15 hour period ending 0300 Feb 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site HOPO3(Hogg Pass) measured 15 inches of snowfall over a 25 hour period ending at 0900 on Feb 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site HOPO3(Hogg Pass) recorded 11 inches over a 19 hour period ending at 2100 on Feb 26th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Via public reports about 22 miles E of Union, hail up to 1.5 inches was reported at 1823 PDT, and 1 inch hail stones one ridge to the east were reported at 1826 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southwest flow aloft and building instability at the surface allowed for thunderstorms to develop throughout the day on May 15th. Several thunderstorms that produced periods of heavy rainfall trained over areas of central OR, which result in localized flooding across portions of Crook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs station reported that heavy rainfall from training thunderstorms between 1700 to 1800 PDT resulted in localized flooding of fields and roads, with minor debris was left behind from receding flood waters. Relayed via a public internet community board, there were several reports from Brasada Ranch resort that homes, roads, and a golf course were damaged by the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the region bringing widespread strong winds and heavy rain to southeast Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station OD111 at Catnip Mountain measured a gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter weather event impacted portions of northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Particularly hard hit was the Portland and Vancouver metro area. A crippling snowstorm impacted the Portland and Vancouver metro area on the afternoon of February 22, continuing into the night. The storm brought widespread 6 to 12 inch snowfall totals to the area. Snowfall distribution was greatly influenced by mesoscale banding. The timing of this event also magnified its impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel sites indicated a 12 hour period in which 1 inch per hour fell while NOHRSC analysis shows general 1 to 2 feet of snow through the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level elongated trough with several shortwave impulses resulted in a period of moderate to heavy snow across the mountain zones. Heaviest snow was observed across the east slopes of the Oregon Cascades and northern Blues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 12 miles east of Weston, OR reported 10 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0940 PDT on March 24th. The Milkshakes SNOTEL measured 11 inches of new snow in a 30 hour period ending at 1400 PDT on March 24th.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper low with deep moisture and low level easterly upslope flow resulted in heavy snow falling across the east slopes of the Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hogg Pass SNOTEL reported 10 inches of new snow in a 12 hour period ending at 0300 PDT on March 28th. A trained spotter 7 miles south southwest of Three Rivers, OR reported 9 inches of new snow at 0755 PDT on March 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening area of low pressure dropping down the BC coastline tracked into the Pacific Northwest, which in combination with the northern edge of an atmospheric river to the south of the CWA lead to heavy mountain snow particularly in the Ochocos and Oregon Cascades. A lee side surface low due to the passing disturbance also enhanced strong winds along the base of the Blue Mountain foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL Ochoco Meadows measured 12 inches of snowfall between 0800 on March 9th and 1500 on March 10th. The SNOTEL Lake Creek R.S. measured 9 inches of snow between 0200 on March 9th and 1000 on March 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening area of low pressure dropping down the BC coastline tracked into the Pacific Northwest, which in combination with the northern edge of an atmospheric river to the south of the CWA lead to heavy mountain snow particularly in the Ochocos and Oregon Cascades. A lee side surface low due to the passing disturbance also enhanced strong winds along the base of the Blue Mountain foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL Hoggs Pass measured 10 inches of snowfall between 1500 on March 9th and 1700 on March 10th. In addition a storm spotter measured 10 inches of snowfall 7 miles SSW of Three River at 1600 on Match 10th which had fallen over the past 24 hrs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter weather event impacted portions of northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Particularly hard hit was the Portland and Vancouver metro area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter weather event impacted portions of northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Particularly hard hit was the Portland and Vancouver metro area. A crippling snowstorm impacted the Portland and Vancouver metro area on the afternoon of February 22, continuing into the night. The storm brought widespread 6 to 12 inch snowfall totals to the area. Snowfall distribution was greatly influenced by mesoscale banding. The timing of this event also magnified its impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began to around 0900 PST, but the heaviest snow fell during the afternoon through early evening hours. Around 6 to 12 inches of snow fell in the Portland/Vancouver metro area by 2230 PST. This resulted in snarled traffic and numerous traffic accidents. Many vehicles were caught up in the traffic jam well into the night.  Portland International Airport closed briefly.  Schools and government offices were closed for up to two days following the event. Over 80 Trimet buses were stranded due to the rapidly deteriorating road conditions. Hundreds of cars were abandoned on area roadways, even on freeways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 15, 2023, the Six Rivers National Forest experienced approximately 150 lightning strikes, which triggered the outbreak of wildfires. There were at least 27 confirmed fires as a result of the lightning strikes Out of 27 confirmed fires, 12 were located on the Gasquet Ranger District in Del Norte County, and they are included in the Smith River Complex. The wildfire was located 7 miles northeast of Gasquet, CA and spread into far southeast Curry County and far southwest Josephine County, near the city of O'Brien.  As of 0530 on 09/01/23, the complex was 4,921 acres, 8 percent contained, had destroyed 12 structures, and cost $41.4M.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 15, 2023, the Six Rivers National Forest experienced approximately 150 lightning strikes, which triggered the outbreak of wildfires. The wildfire was located 7 miles northeast of Gasquet, CA and spread into far southeast Curry County and far southwest Josephine County, near the city of O'Brien.  As of 0530 on 09/01/23, the total complex was 4,921 acres, 8 percent contained, had destroyed 12 structures, and cost $41.4M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tyee Ridge Complex was started by lightning around 812 PM PST on 8/24.  It is located 10 miles west of Sutherlin. It involves 10 wildfires.  As of 530 AM PST on 09/01, it had burned 6,675 acres, was 8% contained, no structures had been destroyed, and it had cost $6.9M.  Numerous homes were threatened, evacuations took place, and there were road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper shortwave moving across the region on August 7th resulted in convective showers and thunderstorms developing across the forecast area. In the morning, a convective shower moved over the town of Ione, Oregon with a heavy downpour that resulted in flash flooding across the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Morrow County Emergency Manager posted pictures via social media post flash flooding through the town. The pictures showed water overflowing drainage ditches and flowing over the roadway, muddy debris leftover across sidewalks and the road within town, and water still flowing into the area creek from the hills surrounding the town. Other photo's from the city of Ione show water and debris that had flowed into local businesses as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture and instability from the southwest led to scattered thunderstorms across the mountainous terrain of eastern Oregon. One of these storms was strong enough to produce large hail across southeastern Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire lookout tower in southeast Grant County reported golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad southwesterly flow pattern advected a plume of mid-level moisture over the region resulted in strong diurnal convection initiating over elevated terrain features in central Oregon and the Washington Cascades initially before progressing northeastward. Strong to severe T-storms, particularly in Central Oregon, produced up to 1.25 inch hail, wind gusts around 59 mph, and heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social Media Report of 1.25in hail that fell just NE of Redmond, OR at 1630L. Time estimate based off radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow aided by an approaching upper level trough facilitated another round of strong diurnal convection during the afternoon hours, starting the central Oregon and along the eastern Mountains before expanding northeastward. Strong to severe T-storms focused in Central Oregon produced up to 1.5 inch hail, strong wind gusts, and heavy downpours that resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media partner reported estimated 1 inch hail fell 6 miles SW of Deschutes River Woods at 1738 PDT. A Public report of hail up to 0.88 inches fell 5 miles NNE of Three Rivers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system offshore the Pacific Northwest produced showers across the eastern mountains throughout the day on November 5th. forecast soundings over this area indicated meager MUCAPE and decent mid-level lapse rates over the eastern mountains of Oregon in the afternoon. This provided enough support for thunderstorm development and a subsequent occurrence of a weak tornado near Canyon City, Or.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximate EF0 tornado path was less than 0.25 miles at Marysville Lane, located east-southeast of Canyon City, Oregon. One tree was uprooted in saturated soil. One manufactured home experienced roof damage, including the removal of roofing |material. An opened hay shelter also saw roofing material removed. A travel-trailer was moved several feet from its original location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing northerly flow reinforced by a cold arctic airmass behind a shortwave trough passage starting October 26th lead to temperatures region-wide dropping well below normal. Overnight temperatures across the lower elevations in the intermountain Pacific Northwest were able to cool well below freezing across much the region for much of the overnight period. This lead to all lower elevation zones experiencing their end of season freeze by the morning of October 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station CW2286 Monument recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 9 hours starting at 0000 PDT. The USCRN site SHRO3 recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 9 hours starting at 2210 PDT. The Oregon DOT station OD200 recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 11 hours starting at 2156 PDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KRDM recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 9 hours ending 0910 PDT. The AWOS KS33 Madras recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 8 hour ending 0855 PDT. The AWOS KBDN recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 7 hours ending 0850 PDT. Most stations in the zone recorded morning low temperatures below 28 degrees.

CZ_NAME: FOOTHILLS OF THE NORTHERN BLUE MOUNTAINS OF OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing northerly flow reinforced by a cold arctic airmass behind a shortwave trough passage starting October 26th lead to temperatures region-wide dropping well below normal. Overnight temperatures across the lower elevations in the intermountain Pacific Northwest were able to cool well below freezing across much the region for much of the overnight period. This lead to all lower elevation zones experiencing their end of season freeze by the morning of October 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KPDT recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 11 hours ending at 1015 PDT. The Oregon DOT station OD191 recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 11 hours ending at 1046 PDT. The mesonet station DW9612 Umapine recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 11 hours ending at 0931 PDT. The Mesonet station EW7174 Adams4w recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 13 hours ending at 1015 PDT. Most stations in the zone recorded morning low temperatrues below 28 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cloud layer forming from upslope processes along the Blue Mountain Foothills resulted in ground fog developing between Cabbage Hill and Poverty Flats in the Northern Blue mountains, or between at least mile posts 219 and 229 along I-84. Reductions in visibilities lead to a significant multi-vehicle accident that closed the freeway down for approximately 8 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 12:30pm PDT, Oregon State Patrol responded to an multi-vehicle incident involving nearly 30 vehicles near mile post 227 on I-84, with two confirmed semi-trucks having caught fire, several motorists reporting minor injuries, and at least one report of a series injury. Multiple Oregon DOT weather stations between mileposts 219 and 229 reported visibilities a quarter mile or less at times before and after the traffic incident. Due to the incident, I-84 was closed both directions between Pendleton and La Grande for over 8 hours according to media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 15, 2023, the Six Rivers National Forest experienced approximately 150 lightning strikes, which triggered the outbreak of wildfires. There were at least 27 confirmed fires as a result of the lightning strikes Out of 27 confirmed fires, 12 were located on the Gasquet Ranger District in Del Norte County, and they are included in the Smith River Complex. The wildfire was located 7 miles northeast of Gasquet, CA and spread into Oregon in far southeast Curry County and far southwest Josephine County, near the city of O'Brien. As of 0530 on 10/02/23, the total complex was 94,606 acres, 87 percent contained, had destroyed 12 structures, and cost $ 129M.

The wildfire was located 7 miles northeast of Gasquet, CA and spread into Oregon in far southeast Curry County and far southwest Josephine County, near the city of O'Brien. As of 0530 on 10/02/23, the total complex was 94,606 acres, 87 percent contained, had destroyed 12 structures, and cost $ 129M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low and associated front approached the north Oregon and south Washington coast. High winds were observed on the immediate coast around the Columbia River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust of 67 mph was reported at Clatsop Spit (3CLO3).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight temperatures were able to cool several degrees below seasonal normal across the Wallowa valley, likely owing to clear skies over the area. This resulted in a season ending freeze affecting the valley area the morning of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several stations in the Wallowa Valley including stations Kb7DZR Joseph, EW8320 Lostine, and CW1102 Wallowa recorded temperatures at or below freezing between 2am PDT and 8am PDT on September 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought extreme heat and heat related impacts to the area. Daily temperature records were set for multiple locations, with the hottest day being Monday August 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of hot weather lead to partner actions being taken and multiple heat related fatalities. High temperatures reached 101 to 108 in Portland between August 13 to 16. Low temperatures only fell to 68 to 73 on those days. Multnomah County reported six suspected heat-related deaths in Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire broke out in South Salem Oregon during a period of dry warm weather with gusty winds. The fire became known as the Liberty Fire and was contained the same day.  About 600 people were evacuated as a result of the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Liberty Fire started around 1350 PM PST August 23, 2023.  About 600 people were evacuated and two homes were reported as damaged or destroyed.  The fire was contained around 1815 PST August 23rd.  Observed wind speeds at the Salem ASOS, not too far to the north of the fire, were out of the N to NNE between 10-15 mph with gusts as high as 18 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire grew to about 25,754 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Salmon Fire started in the afternoon of August 6th near Oakridge Oregon during a period of hot dry weather. The cause of the fire in under investigation. The fire was 95 percent contained at the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salmon Fire started in the afternoon of August 6th near Oakridge Oregon during a period of hot dry weather. The fire grew to 135 acres. The cause of the fire in under investigation. The fire was 95 percent contained at the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river brought heavy rains on December 5th which pushed many streams and rivers to flood stage along the coast, with minor flooding on many smaller streams and creeks across northwest Oregon. After moderate rainfall December 1st through the 4th, a very slow moving front dumped more heavy rain to the northern Oregon coast and coast range mountains. Area of the Coast Range received 12 to 16 inches of rain over the 5 day period. Much of Tillamook County saw flooding impacts, including the Wilson and Trask Rivers, and the Nehalem River near Foss. Other rivers that also reached minor flood stage included the Luckiamute near Suver and the Siletz River at Siletz.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced minor flooding along the Siletz River at Siletz. The river crested at 17.7 feet at 6 pm on December 5th. Flood stage is 16.0 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low in the Gulf of Alaska generated long period swell.  This, combined with shorter period waves associated with an oncoming cold front, produced sneaker waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low in the Gulf of Alaska generated long period swell.  This, combined with shorter period waves associated with an oncoming cold front, produced sneaker waves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal system brought several days of heavy rain, which caused flooding on the Coquille River at Coquille.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille rose above minor flood stage of 21.0 ft at 04/0830 PST, and crested at minor flood stage at 21.24 ft at 04/1900 PST. The river dropped below minor flood stage at 05/0030 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving north over the coastal waters brought a brief period of high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Humbug Mountain sensor on Highway 101 reported high winds with a peak of 50 mph gusting to 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong, slow moving fronts brought a combination of heavy swell and large wind waves to the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Point Saint George buoy (46027) and the Umpqua Offshore buoy (46229) reported swell sufficient to generate high surf. Port Orford reported heavy surf. Surf heights reached 25 to 35 feet, highest on the morning of 12/27.

CZ_NAME: FOOTHILLS OF THE NORTHERN BLUE MOUNTAINS OF OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Dec 27th, the eastern Blue Mountains found itself under a strong pressure gradient owing to strong high pressure situated over the Intermountain West and a closed offshore low centered just west of the Oregon/Washington coast. This intense pressure gradient led to strong winds late on the 26th overnight into the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ODOT station at Cabbage Hill reported a max wind gust of 78 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: La Grande/Union County Airport recorded max wind gust of 63 mph, and ODOT sensor at Charles Reynolds Rest Area along I-84 in Ladd/Pyles Canyon recorded a max wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter weather systems impacted the Pacific Northwest between December 1st through the 3rd. While the systems moving through were fairly quick, they were able to produce heavy snow accumulations across many of the mountain zones across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lake Creek R.S. SNOTEL measured 8 inches of new snow in a 48 hour period ending 3am PST Dec. 3rd. The Starr Ridge SNOTEL measured 7 inches of new snow in a 48 hour period ending 3am Dec. 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of moderate atmospheric rivers brought heavy rain leading to minor river and urban flooding to portions of northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A male age 53, drowned in Bronson Creek, SE of Hillsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging over the region lead to clear skies and dry conditions over the Grande Ronde valley the morning of October 13th. This combination of conditions lead to good radiative cooling occurring overnight, with temperatures at or below 32 degrees for several hours across much of the Grande Ronde valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS KLGD recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for about 3 hours starting at 0410 PDT. The mesonet station CW9803 Elgin reported temperature at or below 32 degrees for over 5 hours starting at 0245 PDT. The mesonet station EW1076 Imbler reported temperatures at or below 32 degrees between 0630 to 0800 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Girl injured by sneaker wave near Yachats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large, long period swell produced a high-end sneaker wave threat to the Oregon coast October 17-19. A young girl was caught in a sneaker wave on the beach near Yachats, OR and was fortunately saved by a stranger.
